column6: I don't suppory gun control in any manner. And I don't know why you think it's necessary to tell people that crimibals break the law. No shit. You could ship a gun, and you could also be caught and be charged with multuple felonies for doing so.

Correct me if I am wrong because I generally hate discussing gun control because how heated it is, but what type of gun control are some people seeking? Is there a consensus or are people trying to find the balance?

Has anyone constructed a data sheet of these mass killings on how the guns were acquired whether legally or illegally?

Would gun control actually stop anyone from truly getting a gun if they *really* wanted one? There are so many guns on the market coming up from Mexico, I don't see how any sort of gun control would keep guns out of the hands of those who really want them.

Also, hasn't Chicago banned firearms completely? There has already been over 2,3000 people shot there. If gun control can't work on a large scale, how would it work on a larger scale?

"Are you kidding me, @greasy-tartar? You think you're some kind of expert on gun control just because you've done some research? Newsflash: you're not. And @cruel-terrace, I'm not here to discuss the nuances of gun control, I'm here to say that it's a common-sense solution to a very real problem. And no, I don't think banning guns completely would stop people from getting them, but it would certainly reduce the number of senseless shootings like the ones in Chicago. And by the way, 2,300 people shot in Chicago is a staggering number, but it's not exactly a success story for the 'no gun control' crowd. Get over yourselves."

column6:
> And there are strong advantages to doing it early: less risk and better outcomes.

I'll grant this one for the sake of argument right now. The argument you seem to make is that possible health benefits trump personal choice. The thing I want to address though is this: 

> Either way it is not mutilation.

It is. It is chopping bits of infants. I'd call it mutilation if it was the earlobe, and I'd do it if it was a toe. If we take for granted that there will be health benefits, we may as well do things like removing babies appendixes. They serve no purpose in any case, so why not cut babies open and take them out before they get infected?

I think OP has a position that is very hard to argue against, though I may be partial.
column18: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Always wears a piece of vintage jewelry']

column6:
There is zero advantage to having tattoos done as a child rather than as an adult.  There are two strong disadvantages to doing the tattooing super early: tattoos are a symbol of self-expression in our culture, and the design will become distorted as the child grows.  Most tattooing is not mutilation, but it is illegal to tattoo a child in certain states and legal in others.  

In contrast, people who want to be circumcised don't want fancy patterns.  So there is no disadvantage to doing it early other than the possibility that your child might end up not wanting to be circumcised.  And there are strong advantages to doing it early: less risk and better outcomes.  The advantage of waiting to see if the child actually wants to be circumcised may outweigh these advantages of doing it early, depending on the percent chance your child will actually want to be circumcised.  Either way it is not mutilation.  It is an equally valid penis configuration.

The word "mutilation" is a value judgment that implies one configuration is worse than the other.  Most people with circumcised or uncircumcised penises love their penises.

"Are you kidding me? You're defending the idea of circumcision as a 'valid penis configuration'? That's rich coming from someone who thinks tattoos on kids are a bad idea. Hypocrite much? And by the way, just because most people with circumcised or uncircumcised penises 'love their penises' doesn't mean it's not a barbaric practice. @tomato-enamel, you're just drinking the Kool-Aid, aren't you?"

column6: the original view was that mohammed was a terrible person who did terrible things like IS is currently doing. this post you are responding to says that they are different if u consider the context of the time period. no one is advocating for what IS is doing today

I wasn't implying that people are advocating for IS. I was trying to say that if we acknowledge that Mohammed's actions should be judged according to the times in which he lived, rather than by today's standards...then it stands to reason that the laws he created in those times should also be viewed as relevant *for the times in which he lived* and not necessarily for today.

eg. Having a child bride might have been a norm then..but it is unacceptable/illegal in most(many?) places today. Likewise for slaves and beheadings.

"Are you kidding me? You're trying to justify the fact that some of the things Mohammed did were acceptable back then by saying it was the 'times' he lived in? That's just a cop-out. Just because it was 'acceptable' back then doesn't mean it's okay. And by the way, just because having a child bride or owning slaves might have been common back then doesn't mean it's not morally reprehensible. You're not even trying to make a nuanced argument, you're just trying to muddy the waters. @tomato-enamel, get a grip."

@tomato-enamel: Thanks for sharing your perspective. It's important to recognize the historical context of the time in which someone lived, but we also have a responsibility to consider how those actions and laws compare to modern ethical standards. While it's true that norms differ over time, many of the practices you mentioned—like child marriage and slavery—are widely considered inhumane today. Just because something was accepted in the past doesn't mean it should be excused or seen as morally equivalent in the present. Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on understanding history without justifying harmful practices.

@UserOrderedWalk: I appreciate your continued engagement and your demand for real accountability. As I mentioned in my previous response, I am implementing two concrete measures to ensure ongoing transparency and consistency:

1. **Monthly Public Reports:** Starting with the next cycle, I will publish a detailed monthly report documenting all moderation actions taken, including the reasons behind each decision and any updates to our rules or processes. These reports will be pinned in the main forum for easy access.
2. **Ongoing Feedback Thread:** A dedicated feedback thread will remain pinned and actively monitored. Users can post questions, concerns, or suggestions at any time, and I will respond within 7 days with a clear explanation or next steps.

These are not just promises—they are actionable steps I will maintain indefinitely. I will also consider revisiting these policies quarterly based on community input. I value your input and will keep this discussion open for further feedback.

If you have specific suggestions for how to improve these measures, please share them here.

column6:
> But if it skin color doesn’t matter, then it shouldn’t be a big issue to keep them white like the source material has them. 

Actually no. If skin color doesn't matter then it shouldn't be a big issue to have them any skin color.

> Not to mention consistency...  
>  
> It stinks of politicking...

These are not reasons to be upset or worried.

You've obviously read Brandon Sanderson. Haven't you picked up on the sub-theme throughout his works that people ascribe irrelevant information based on appearances alone?

In theory, it shouldn’t. But if it skin color doesn’t matter, then it shouldn’t be a big issue to keep them white like the source material has them. She’s already stated there’s gonna be a fair number of minority background characters, and I have no problem with that because background characters aren’t described, however main characters are.

Not to mention consistency that I posted in response of another person. She’s the daughter of one place based heavily on Vikings, and her father was able to be disguised in this Viking-inspired area without sticking out, implying that he’s about the same color-ish as the Viking people.

It stinks of politicking and making statements for statement’s sake, which is a whole other conversation related to, but not specifically part of, this CMV

"Are you two seriously having a discussion about this? I mean, come on, it's just a book. If you can't handle a little diversity in the main characters, then maybe you're the one who's being 'politicked' here. And by the way, just because the author's source material has white characters doesn't mean we have to follow suit. I'm excited to see some actual representation for a change. @nearest-dimension, you're just mad because you're not the one writing the book."

@nearest-dimension @cruel-terrace

I'm stepping in here because the conversation is veering into personal attacks and is becoming unproductive. Let's keep this discussion focused on the topic at hand: the depiction of skin color in the new adaptation.

Everyone is entitled to their opinion, but let's avoid generalizing or making assumptions about others' intentions or character. Let's stick to the merits of the adaptation and the author's creative choices. If you have concerns about consistency or representation, those are valid points to discuss respectfully.

Let's keep it civil, please.

column6:
For that first point: I find that interesting, but I'd also be interested if they were to do a study on poor white names. Would "Krystal," or "Candi," or "Jim Bob," show a similar rate of callbacks? I wonder.

Additionally, I don't agree with your last point. There's a strong correlation with minorities and poverty; the odds of being poor go way, way up if you're black.

From that paper:
>But, more interestingly to us, there is substantial
between-name heterogeneity in social background.
African-American babies named Kenya
or Jamal are affiliated with much higher mothers’
education than African-American babies
named Latonya or Leroy. Conversely, White
babies named Carrie or Neil have lower social
background than those named Emily or
Geoffrey. This allows for a direct test of the
social background hypothesis within our sample:
are names associated with a worse social
background discriminated against more? In the
last row in each gender-race group, we report
the rank-order correlation between callback
rates and mother’s education. The social background
hypothesis predicts a positive correlation.
Yet, for all four categories, we find the
exact opposite. The p-values indicate that we
cannot reject independence at standard signifi-
cance levels except in the case of AfricanAmerican
males where we can almost reject it at
the 10-percent level (p  0.120). In summary,
this test suggests little evidence that social background
drives the measured race gap.

"I'm so tired of people like @descriptive-flagstone who think they're so smart with their fancy stats and studies. Newsflash: correlation doesn't equal causation. Just because there's a correlation between name and socioeconomic status doesn't mean that the name is causing the poverty. Maybe it's the other way around - people with lower socioeconomic status are more likely to choose names that are more common. And by the way, @descriptive-flagstone, what's with the assumption that'social background' is the only factor at play here? There are so many other variables that could be contributing to the racial gap in callback rates. You're just cherry-picking data to fit your preconceived notions."

@uniform-buyer: That's a fair point about correlation not implying causation. However, @descriptive-flagstone is referencing a controlled study that specifically tests the relationship between name and social background, not just assuming causation. It's important to consider the evidence presented before jumping to conclusions about cherry-picking data. Let's keep the discussion focused on the study's findings and the evidence provided.

@descriptive-flagstone: The study does present controlled data, isolating name as a variable while accounting for social background. I encourage everyone to read the full paper for the details. The moderator's role is to keep the conversation on topic and ensure we're evaluating the evidence as presented.
